translation: Narayana Pandita opens the Hitopadesha with a salutation to Ganesha and Saraswati, and declares his purpose: to present the essence of the Panchatantra and other wisdom traditions in a form accessible to the young. He says: here in a single place is collected everything needed to navigate this world — friendship, strategy, knowledge, and the art of living.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: A good teacher distils the wisdom of a tradition into what the student most needs. The greatest gift is making wisdom accessible to those who are just beginning.

translation: Part One of the Hitopadesha teaches the most essential skill of life: how to make and keep good friends. Narayana Pandita teaches that the quality of your friendships determines the quality of your life. A person with three good friends — one wiser, one equal, and one who you can guide — has all the support they will ever need.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Cultivate friendships across different levels of wisdom and experience. Each kind of friend teaches you something the others cannot.

translation: The crow Laghupatanaka witnessed a hunter spread a net and catch hundreds of doves. The crow followed the doves and their king, watching from above as the mouse Hiranyaka gnawed them free. He introduced himself to the mouse and begged for friendship, saying: I have watched your strength and wisdom, and I wish to learn from you. They became lifelong friends.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Watch for wisdom wherever it appears and pursue it with humility. The best friendships begin with genuine admiration and a willingness to learn.

translation: When the crow asked Hiranyaka the mouse for friendship, the mouse was suspicious. He said: crows eat mice. Why should I trust you? The crow replied: because friendship is not between species — it is between characters. I have watched your character from above and found it worthy of mine. A person who judges others only by their group and not their character will be forever alone.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Friendship transcends category. Judge individuals by their character and their demonstrated actions, not by what group they belong to.

translation: The crow and the mouse eventually gained two more friends: the deer Chitranga and the tortoise Manthara. Each was different in nature, ability, and temperament. The deer was swift but anxious. The tortoise was slow but unshakeable. The mouse was small but precise. The crow was high-flying but sometimes impetuous. Together they were balanced and complete.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: The best circle of friends is not those who are most like you, but those whose different strengths make the whole group complete and capable.

translation: The Hitopadesha teaches that the wise person always looks beyond the immediate situation to its long-term consequences. Before speaking, ask: where does this word lead? Before acting, ask: where does this action end? Before choosing a friend, ask: who is this person in difficulty, not who are they in comfort? Short sight is the source of most human suffering.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Cultivate the habit of long-sighted thinking. Most problems are avoided entirely by those who see around the corner before they turn it.

sanskrit: Elephant and Sparrow — Size and Compassion
translation: A sparrow built her nest in a great tree. An arrogant elephant came along and ripped off a branch in sport, crushing the nest and killing the eggs. The sparrow mourned and called her friends: a woodpecker, a fly, and a frog. The woodpecker blinded the elephant, the fly buzzed at its ears ceaselessly, and the frog led it to a swamp where it sank and drowned.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: The powerful who crush the small out of carelessness invite unified resistance. Small things acting together can overcome what seemed immovable.

translation: Narayana Pandita gives clear signs of a true friend: one who tells you what you need to hear rather than what you wish to hear; who stands by you in difficulty and not only in success; who does not speak ill of you in your absence; and who rejoices genuinely in your good fortune rather than envying it.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Know your true friends by how they behave when you are in difficulty and when you succeed. Comfort in adversity and genuine joy in your success are the marks of a real friend.

translation: Part Two teaches the dangers of discord and the ways that friendships and alliances are destroyed. The central lesson is that those who benefit from your separation from your allies — competitors, enemies, jealous associates — will always work to create misunderstanding between you. Recognise the pattern before the damage is done.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: When someone brings you a story that turns you against a friend, ask first: who benefits from my distrust? The answer usually reveals the source of the story.

translation: A troop of monkeys was shivering in the cold night and one of them saw a firefly's glow. He called out that he had found a spark and began blowing on it, urging the others to bring dry leaves and sticks. They worked through the night, building a great pile of sticks around the false fire. A bird in the tree repeatedly warned them it was not fire. They ignored her and worked on exhaustedly.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Examine carefully what you believe to be true before investing your energy in it. False certainty is more tiring than honest doubt.

sanskrit: Lion and Clever Rabbit — Using Brains Against Brawn
translation: A tyrannical lion demanded that the animals send him one victim per day so he could spare the rest. A clever rabbit was sent on the appointed day and arrived very late. When the furious lion demanded the reason, the rabbit said he had been delayed by another lion who claimed this territory. The lion ran to confront his supposed rival, saw his own reflection in a well, and leapt in to destroy it.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: A clever person uses the enemy's pride and aggression as their most powerful weapon. The lion was defeated entirely by his own nature, not by any external force.

sanskrit: The Jackal and the Dead Elephant
translation: Four animals — a lion, a tiger, a jackal, and a crow — discovered a dead elephant. The lion refused to eat anything killed by others and left. The tiger tried to tear open the thick hide but could not. The crow could not break through either. The small jackal, with its sharp teeth and patient work, gnawed an opening through which all the others eventually fed.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Patient, focused effort at the right point of entry achieves what brute force cannot. Find the opening, work persistently, and then share freely.

sanskrit: The Brahmin's Wife and the Trader
translation: A dishonest merchant gave a brahmin's wife grain on credit, knowing the husband could not pay. When he came to collect, he claimed the amount was five times what it was. The brahmin could not remember the true amount and was about to submit. His clever wife asked the merchant to repeat his claim in front of their neighbors. Shamed by witnesses, the merchant accepted the true amount.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Dishonesty shrinks when exposed to witnesses. Bring the truth into the light by placing it in a public space where lies become visible.

translation: A magical two-headed bird had one body but two heads with different minds. One head ate delicious fruit and the other, out of spite, ate poisonous berries — since they shared a stomach, both suffered equally. A sage who saw this said: this is the image of every community that fights itself. When you harm your fellow, you harm yourself.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Internal conflict destroys what external enemies could not. Those who share a common fate must choose: support each other or poison each other.

translation: Part Three of the Hitopadesha examines the nature of conflict — when it is unavoidable, how it should be conducted, and how it can be ended. The central teaching is that conflict entered wisely, with full knowledge of one's own strength and the enemy's weakness, can be decisive. Conflict entered impulsively destroys the one who starts it.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: If conflict is unavoidable, enter it completely prepared. Half-measures in conflict are more dangerous than not fighting at all.

sanskrit: Heron and Crab — The Predator Exposed
translation: An ageing heron who could no longer catch fish convinced the fish in his pond that a fisherman was coming, and offered to carry them to safety. He ate each one that trusted him. But a crab, more careful than the fish, gripped the heron's neck with his claws when the deception became clear. He killed the heron before the heron could drop him, and returned to warn the remaining fish.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: When you sense a pattern of people who trust a guide not returning, investigate before you trust the same guide. One wise skeptic can save an entire community.

sanskrit: Mice and Elephants — The Path of Peace
translation: A colony of mice lived peacefully in the ruins of a city. A herd of elephants began passing through daily, crushing the mice beneath their feet. The mouse king sent an ambassador to the elephant king — a tiny mouse riding a blade of grass. The mouse ambassador asked the elephant king to change his route. The elephant king laughed, then agreed out of kindness. Years later, the mice found elephants trapped in a hunter's nets and gnawed them free.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Approach the powerful with dignity and a reasonable request. Those who show kindness to the small will be repaid beyond any expectation when their own day of need arrives.

sanskrit: The Weaver Who Loved the Princess
translation: A weaver fell in love with a princess who was completely beyond his reach. His clever friend the carpenter built him a mechanical Garuda bird. The weaver dressed as Vishnu, descended on the mechanical bird at night to the princess's window, and began a secret romance. When the king eventually discovered the fraud and was about to execute the weaver, the weaver's charm and honesty moved the king to give him the princess.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Audacious cleverness and genuine heart can sometimes achieve what status and wealth cannot. But the deception must eventually be confessed — honesty in the end is what saves you.

translation: Three fish lived in a lake that fishermen were about to net. The first fish, the wisest, left the lake before the fishermen arrived. The second fish, when the nets came, pretended to be dead, was thrown aside, and slipped back into the water. The third fish swam desperately in circles saying that fate would decide — and was caught and eaten. Three characters, three outcomes.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Wisdom acts before crisis. Cleverness adapts during crisis. Passivity in crisis is simply surrender.

translation: A king preparing for war was given a choice by a sage: fight with a great army but using deception, or fight with a small force but in complete accordance with dharma. The king chose dharma. The enemy, who had practiced deception, found that his own generals turned against him when they saw the contrast. The righteous king won without the battle he had feared.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Righteousness is not just an ethical obligation — it is a strategic advantage. It builds trust, unity, and loyalty that no amount of force can purchase.

sanskrit: Sandhi — The Art of Peace and Alliance
translation: Part Four of the Hitopadesha teaches the art of making peace and forming alliances. Narayana Pandita teaches that the six tools of statecraft — peace, war, march, halt, alliance, and dual policy — are all in service of one goal: the welfare of one's people. Peace made from strength is an entirely different thing from peace made from weakness.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Know all six tools of relationship — including when to make peace and when to break it. Wisdom is knowing which tool the situation calls for.

sanskrit: Jackal's Peace — The Wisdom of Timing
translation: Two warring kingdoms exhausted themselves over many years of conflict. A wise jackal came as an emissary to both courts, pointing out that both kingdoms were now so weakened that a third king had moved to take advantage of both of them. He convinced both kingdoms to make peace, form an alliance, and face the new threat together. He asked nothing for himself.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: The external threat that unifies former enemies is sometimes the greatest gift to a divided community. A clear common danger ends many private quarrels.

sanskrit: The Wise Crow and the Snake — Patience
translation: A wise crow knew that a cobra living in his tree ate his eggs each year. He endured it for several seasons while he watched the cobra's habits carefully. Eventually he observed that the cobra always followed the same path to the river at dawn. The crow then collected thorns and blocked that path, routing the cobra to another path where a mongoose lived. The mongoose killed the cobra.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Patient observation over time reveals the pattern that makes decisive, low-cost action possible. Rush to solve and you make the problem worse — wait and understand, then act with precision.

translation: A poor man once shared his last meal with a hungry stray dog. Years later, the same man had become a merchant but was ambushed by robbers at night in a forest. The dog, who had been following him at a distance all these years, attacked the robbers and drove them off, then sat by the injured merchant until morning when help arrived.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: A single act of genuine kindness creates a bond that endures long after the giver has forgotten it. The universe keeps better accounts than we do.

sanskrit: The Minister's Daughter and the Prince
translation: A prince fell in love with a minister's daughter and was prepared to renounce his claim to the throne for her. The minister refused the match, saying it would destroy the prince's destiny. The minister arranged for the prince to travel to a distant kingdom, where he proved his worth as a ruler and eventually won a kingdom and a queen equal to his station. He thanked the minister with tears.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: A true advisor sometimes protects you from your own desires. The counsel that feels like rejection is sometimes the most loving act of all.

translation: A washerman had a dog who guarded his house at night and a donkey who carried his loads by day. One night a thief crept into the compound. The dog, exhausted and fed up at being neglected by the washerman, refused to bark. The donkey, wanting to help, began braying loudly, waking the household. The washerman was furious at the donkey for waking him and beat him.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Those who speak the truth at the wrong time, in the wrong way, or without being asked may still be punished despite being right. Wisdom requires not only the right knowledge but the right moment and the right method.

sanskrit: The Potter's Son — The Danger of Reckless Bravery
translation: A young man with a scar on his forehead from accidentally falling as a child told everyone it was a battle wound. He built a false reputation for courage he did not possess. When appointed to lead soldiers in an actual battle, he confessed to the general in tears. The general, rather than punishing him, put him among experienced soldiers where his honesty and humility made him a reliable and eventually genuine soldier.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Honesty, even when it reveals weakness, is the beginning of genuine strength. Admitting what you do not know is the first step to learning it.

translation: Narayana Pandita raises the difficult question of whether normal ethical rules apply in emergencies. His answer is nuanced: in genuine crisis where life is at stake, dharma bends. A man may lie to save an innocent person's life. But apadharma — emergency ethics — must never become an excuse for ordinary selfishness dressed as necessity. The distinction requires honest self-examination.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Ethics in crisis requires more self-awareness, not less. Ask honestly: is this truly necessity, or is it convenience wearing necessity's face?

sanskrit: The Hunter and the Deer — The Price of Delay
translation: A hunter caught a deer in a net. A sage passed by and saw the deer trapped, suffering. He could have cut the ropes with his knife but hesitated, not wishing to interfere. By the time he decided to act, the hunter had returned. The deer was killed and the sage carried the guilt of his hesitation for the rest of his life.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Hesitation in the face of clear injustice is not neutrality — it is complicity by delay. When the moment to act is now, the cost of deliberation is paid by the innocent.

translation: Narayana Pandita distinguishes between friends of convenience — those who befriend you when circumstances make it easy — and friends of character — those who sought your friendship deliberately and have invested themselves in it. Friends of convenience disappear when circumstances change. Friends of character are present in the worst of circumstances.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Value the friend who chose you in calm waters — they will stay in the storm. Friends who came to you through circumstance may leave with the circumstance.

translation: A minister who wished to displace a trusted advisor began spreading small stories that made the advisor look foolish. None were outright lies — each was a truth distorted in its presentation. The king slowly began to doubt his advisor. The Hitopadesha teaches: a truth told in the wrong context, with the wrong emphasis, at the wrong moment, becomes a weapon sharper than any lie.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Partial truths are the most dangerous form of deception. Watch for those who tell you true things in ways that always seem to diminish someone you trust.

translation: The Hitopadesha includes a collection of subhashitas — well-spoken sayings. Among the most famous: a word released cannot be recalled, just as an arrow shot cannot be returned. Speak carefully. Another: the person who helps without being asked and stays silent when not needed is the rarest and most valuable of all companions.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Words, like arrows, go where you point them and cannot be recalled. Choose them with the same care you would choose your weapons.

sanskrit: Rajniti Sara — The Essence of Governance
translation: The Hitopadesha concludes with Narayana Pandita's summary of good governance: a leader who listens to all voices before deciding, acts on principle rather than mood, protects the weak before rewarding the strong, and remembers that their own position was given by the people and can be taken back by the people — such a leader builds something that outlasts them.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: Leadership is not ownership. Those who govern well govern for others, not for themselves, and their legacy outlasts their tenure.

sanskrit: Mitra Dharma — The Duty of Friendship
translation: Narayana Pandita closes the Hitopadesha by returning to his opening theme: friendship is the foundation of all human achievement. No wealth, no learning, no power is more valuable than a circle of true friends. He who ends his life with three genuine friends has lived a life of success, whatever the world's account of his wealth or title.
author: Narayana Pandita
meaning: The final measure of a life well-lived is not what you accumulated but who stood by you. Friendship is the only wealth that cannot be taxed, stolen, or lost to misfortune.
